title: Radiation Hazards and Exposure Control in Daily Operations
narrationScript: Everything you have learned about radiation safety comes together on the job site, where a source that can deliver a lethal dose rides in the crew truck all shift. First, name the hazard honestly. The exposure device is the largest source you will ever stand beside, and the guide tube and source stop are where people get hurt — that is where a source can hang up outside its shield without anyone feeling a thing. Then control the hazard in the right order. The strongest protections are built into the equipment and the site, not the ones that depend on a tired worker. Engineering and shielding first, then the boundary and written procedures, then time, distance, and shielding at the source, then your monitoring gear, and last your own attention. That is the hierarchy of controls. After every exposure, retract the source and confirm it shielded with the meter. Most serious accidents are not exotic: a disconnected source, a skipped survey, a dead meter, caught too late. Know your procedures cold and keep the meter in your hand.

sections.heading: The Hazard You Actually Work Next To
sections.body:
By the time you reach daily operations, the abstractions are over. An industrial radiographic exposure device holds a sealed gamma source — typically **iridium-192**, **cobalt-60**, or **selenium-75** — that emits continuously and cannot be switched off. It is, by a wide margin, the largest radiation source most technicians will ever stand beside, and a few feet of separation is often all that lies between a routine shot and a serious overexposure.

The hazard is not spread evenly. In normal operation the source is locked in the shielded position inside the device, and external dose rates are low. The danger concentrates in two places and two moments:

- **The source itself when it leaves the shield** — running out through the **guide tube** to the **source stop (collimator)** during an exposure. Here the unshielded source produces dose rates of **hundreds of R/h within a foot or two**.
- **Any moment the source is outside the shield but not where you think it is** — a source that fails to crank fully back, a disconnected source assembly left in the guide tube, or a source stuck partway. The device looks identical either way.

This is whole-body, deeply penetrating external exposure, and the part most likely to be injured is whatever a worker puts closest to a stuck source — usually the **hands**. Nothing about it can be sensed. Only the survey meter knows where the source is.

sections.heading: Control the Hazard in the Right Order
sections.body:
It is not enough to know the controls; you have to apply them in the order of strength. Radiation protection follows a **hierarchy of controls** — the same logic used across occupational safety. Protections that remove the hazard or work automatically, regardless of human behavior, sit at the top. Protections that depend on a person doing the right thing every time sit at the bottom, where they are weakest.

From strongest to weakest:

- **Engineering and shielding** — the device's own shielding, source collimators, exposure rooms with interlocks, and beam direction. Built in, always working, no decision required.
- **Administrative controls** — the posted **high-radiation-area** boundary, restricted-area access control, written **operating and emergency procedures**, and the survey routine. These govern how the job is run.
- **Time, distance, and shielding worked at the source** — minimizing exposure time, maximizing distance (the inverse-square law is your most powerful field tool), and adding portable shielding when practical.
- **Personnel monitoring** — the survey meter, the **alarming ratemeter**, the direct-reading **pocket dosimeter**, and the badge/TLD. These do not prevent dose; they detect and warn.
- **Worker care and attention** — the last layer, and the one most likely to fail under fatigue and schedule pressure.

The lesson is blunt: a barrier rope and a door interlock protect a crew more reliably than a careful person, because they do not get tired. Lean on the strong controls first.

sections.caption: The hierarchy of exposure controls in radiography, from the strongest engineering and shielding measures at the top down to worker attention at the base — stronger controls do not depend on human behavior.

sections.title: After every exposure: retract, then survey
sections.body: The single most important habit in field radiography is to **return the source to the fully shielded position and confirm it with the survey meter after every exposure** — before anyone approaches the device, the guide tube, or the part. A source reads as 'home' on the crank handle but may be hung up in the guide tube; only the meter tells the truth. A radiographer who walks toward a device on the assumption that the source is shielded, without a meter reading, is doing the exact thing that has caused the worst injuries in this trade.

sections.heading: Survey Before, During, and After
sections.body:
The survey is not paperwork — it is how you locate an invisible hazard in real time, and it brackets every exposure:

- **Before** — confirm the meter is calibrated, battery-checked, and responding to its check source. Survey the device and area to establish a baseline and verify the source is shielded before you begin setting up.
- **During** — establish and survey the **restricted-area boundary** so the dose rate at the boundary stays within the limit for the area's classification, and confirm no one is inside the high-radiation area. The boundary is set by measured dose rate, never by guesswork or a tape measure alone.
- **After** — survey the device, the guide tube, and the source stop to **prove the source fully returned to its shielded position**. This is the survey that catches a disconnected or stuck source. Only after this survey is the area cleared and the device handled.

The instrument that does this work is the **portable survey meter** (an ion chamber or G-M survey instrument reading in mR/h or R/h), backed by your **alarming ratemeter** for continuous warning and your **pocket dosimeter** for accumulated dose. A survey performed with an uncalibrated, un-responding, or pegged-out-of-range meter is not a survey at all.

sections.caption: Common radiography exposure hazards, the failure behind each, and the control that stops it.
rows: Source runs out to make an exposure
Intense field at the source stop and along the guide tube
Restricted-area boundary set by survey; maximize distance; minimize time
Source hangs up in guide tube; device looks normal
Post-exposure survey of device and guide tube every time
Source left exposed in the guide tube after the connector fails
Survey before approach; never approach without a meter
Unplanned dose to a worker or bystander
Posted boundary, barriers, and continuous-survey/area monitoring
Meter dead or out of range
Pre-use battery and check-source test; correct range selection
Severe localized extremity dose in seconds
Remote handling; never touch a source or place hands near the stop

sections.heading: Operating and Emergency Procedures: Why They Exist
sections.body:
Every licensed radiography operation runs on **written operating and emergency procedures**, and they are not bureaucratic decoration. Each one is the residue of an accident that already happened to someone else. The serious incidents in this industry are rarely exotic — they cluster around a short list: a **source that did not retract**, a **survey that was skipped**, a **meter that was dead or ignored**, and a **boundary that was not maintained**. Procedures exist to interrupt exactly those failures.

Operating procedures define the normal sequence — survey, set the boundary, make the exposure, retract, survey again, log it — so that the safe path is also the routine path. **Emergency procedures** define what to do when the source does **not** behave: a source stuck out of its shield is a radiological emergency, and the correct response is built on the same physics you already know.

- **Maximize distance** — back away immediately; do not stay near the source trying to understand the problem.
- **Minimize time** — no one approaches the source longer than the procedure allows, and approaches are planned and rehearsed.
- **Use shielding and remote tools** — never bare hands.
- **Account for everyone, secure the area, and notify** the Radiation Safety Officer and the chain the procedure names; do not attempt unauthorized recovery.

Know your own procedures cold before you need them. In an emergency there is no time to read.

sections.caption: The survey-bracketed exposure cycle: survey before, set and survey the restricted-area boundary during the shot, retract the source, then survey the device and guide tube to prove the source returned to its shielded position.

sections.title: ALARA is the standard, not the dose limit
sections.body: Staying under the regulatory dose limit is the floor, not the goal. **ALARA — as low as reasonably achievable** — is the operating standard, and in daily work it is concrete: take the survey, stand at the boundary instead of inside it, let the inverse-square law do the work, finish the shot and move out, and keep the source shielded except during the exposure itself. Every avoidable rem you do not receive is the whole point of the job done right.

figures.title: Hierarchy of exposure controls in radiography

interactive.title: A Shot Goes Wrong
interactive.intro: You are the radiographer on a two-person crew shooting pipe welds with an Ir-192 projector at a fabrication yard. Your calibrated survey meter, alarming ratemeter, and pocket dosimeter are with you. Make the calls a safe radiographer makes.

steps.prompt: You arrive at the shot location to set up. Before you do anything else with the exposure device, what comes first?
options: Crank the source out so you can confirm the projector works
Battery-check the survey meter, confirm it responds to its check source, and survey the device and area to verify the source is shielded
Set the boundary ropes at a fixed 50 feet and start shooting
Connect the guide tube and load the film without surveying
steps.feedback: Nothing happens before the meter is proven and a baseline survey is taken. An uncalibrated or unresponsive meter cannot locate the source, and you confirm the source is shielded before you ever handle the device.

steps.prompt: You set the restricted-area boundary. What determines where the boundary rope goes?
options: A standard distance memorized from training, the same for every job
Wherever the customer's other trades are not working
The measured dose rate — the boundary is placed so the dose rate there stays within the limit for the area's classification
As close to the source as possible to save space on the job site
steps.feedback: The boundary is set by survey-measured dose rate, not by a fixed tape-measure number. Source strength, collimation, and geometry change shot to shot, so the meter — not a memorized distance — defines the line.

steps.prompt: After the exposure you crank the source back. The handle reads 'home,' but your survey meter at the device shows the field is still high. What is happening, and what do you do?
options: The meter is wrong because the handle says home — approach and pack up the device
The source is likely stuck or disconnected in the guide tube. Stay back, keep everyone out, and treat it as a radiological emergency per procedure
Re-zero the dosimeter and take the shot again
Walk up to the guide tube and pull the source back by hand
steps.feedback: A high reading after retraction means the source did not return to its shield — exactly the failure the post-exposure survey exists to catch. The handle can lie; the meter cannot. This is an emergency: maximize distance, secure the area, and follow your emergency procedure.

steps.prompt: With the source stuck out of its shield, your partner suggests reaching in quickly with gloved hands to free the source assembly. Your response?
options: Agree — gloves provide enough protection for a quick reach
Refuse: never place hands near an unshielded source. Maximize distance, minimize time, use remote tools and shielding, account for everyone, and notify the RSO per the emergency procedure
Send your partner in since it is their idea
Stand next to the device and study the mechanism until you understand the jam
steps.feedback: Hands near a stuck source is how the worst extremity injuries in this trade happen — gloves stop nothing. The emergency response is distance, minimal planned time, shielding and remote handling, area control, and RSO notification. Unauthorized hands-on recovery is never the answer.

checkQuestions.question: In the hierarchy of exposure controls, which type of control is considered the strongest?
options: Worker care and attention to the job
The direct-reading pocket dosimeter
Engineering and shielding built into the equipment and site
Reminding the crew to be careful at the start of each shift
checkQuestions.explanation: Engineering and shielding sit at the top of the hierarchy because they work automatically and do not depend on a person behaving correctly. Monitoring devices and worker attention are weaker, lower-tier controls that detect or react rather than prevent.

checkQuestions.question: What is the single most important action to take after every radiographic exposure?
options: Immediately move the projector to the next shot location
Return the source to its fully shielded position and confirm it with the survey meter before approaching the device
Record the exposure time in the shot log
Re-zero the pocket dosimeter for the next exposure
checkQuestions.explanation: A source can read as 'home' on the crank handle yet remain hung up in the guide tube. The post-exposure survey with the meter is what proves the source actually retracted, and it must happen before anyone approaches.

checkQuestions.question: Where should the restricted-area (high-radiation-area) boundary be placed during an exposure?
options: At a fixed distance memorized in training, identical for every job
As close to the source as the crew can tolerate
At the edge of the customer's property line
Wherever the survey meter shows the dose rate has fallen to the limit for that area classification
checkQuestions.explanation: The boundary is defined by measured dose rate, not a fixed distance. Source strength, collimation, and geometry vary from shot to shot, so only a survey can place the boundary correctly.

checkQuestions.question: A source is stuck in the exposed position out of its shield. Which set of actions reflects the correct emergency response?
options: Reach in quickly with gloved hands to free the source assembly
Maximize distance, minimize time near the source, use shielding and remote tools, secure the area, and notify the RSO per procedure
Take another exposure to see whether the source frees itself
Stand close to the device and study the mechanism until the jam is understood
checkQuestions.explanation: A stuck source is a radiological emergency handled with the same physics you already know — distance, time, and shielding — plus area control and RSO notification. Gloves provide no meaningful protection, and unauthorized hands-on recovery is never acceptable.

checkQuestions.question: Most serious industrial radiography accidents share a common root. Which best describes it?
options: Routine failures such as a source that did not retract, a skipped survey, or a dead or ignored meter — caught too late
Exotic equipment failures no procedure could anticipate
Gamma radiation making the inspected weld radioactive
Naturally occurring background radiation exceeding the dose limit
checkQuestions.explanation: Serious incidents cluster around a short, mundane list — unretracted or disconnected sources, missed surveys, and meters that were dead or ignored. Written operating and emergency procedures exist precisely to interrupt these predictable failures.

references: ANSI/ASNT CP-105-2024 Radiation Safety Outline §7.0 (safety and health; radiation hazards, methods of controlling exposure, operation and emergency procedures)
10 CFR Part 34 (Licenses for Industrial Radiography) — operating and emergency procedures, surveys, and restricted-area requirements, described generically; your license and procedures govern
10 CFR Part 20 (Standards for Protection Against Radiation) — occupational dose limits, ALARA, and high-radiation-area controls
Typical hierarchy-of-controls and ALARA practice in industrial radiography health physics
